title: Would Gendo need the whole Rei for his plan
display_title: Would Gendo need the whole Rei for his plan
wikitext:
[]: Would Gendo need the "whole Rei" for his plan?             Quote Post by Seoul Gamer » Mon May 28, 2012 6:15 am I recall someone saying that if Rei hadn't reclaimed the portion of her soul that was in her Eva, that it would mean Gendo would have had to have had Unit 00 down in Terminal Dogma as well for his plan. Would this really be necessary-that Rei would have to have both parts of her soul? If this was true then Gendo would have been looking for excuses to bump off Rei along with Unit 00, not showing concern for her the way he did. In other words, if it was Rei II in Terminal Dogma instead of Rei III, would it have made any difference at all to Gendo's plan?

[]:             Quote Post by User-iel » Mon May 28, 2012 11:19 am Perhaps the screenplay never answered this precise question, but I can turn it around for an amusing cliché. Gendo got a whole lot more Rei than he was counting on. Essentially, he ran afoul because Rei was too complete by this point, even if just by a little bit. If he had the choice to not have her as an independent person, he would easily have gained his outcome. 'Soul math' is not strongly described either, but perhaps the only important part of the "recipe" that is GNR was Rei II.

[]:             Quote Post by Drabant » Sat Jun 30, 2012 6:40 pm That could very well be the case, but isn't Gendo in a position to reclaim EVA00's soul as he pleases? All he need to do is destroy or purge the core. Though this could mean he would have to euthanize Rei.... What a horrible train of thought, lets just be happy that Rei like Kaworu got to choose their bodily deaths on their own terms. User-iel wrote: Gendo got a whole lot more Rei than he was counting on.

[]:             Quote Post by Tribblepoo » Tue Jul 24, 2012 1:38 am It could also be that it simply didn't matter. The forces involved in 3I were doubtlessly powerful enough to yank Lilith's soul from Unit 00 if a part of it were still there. If this is true, the question is did Gendo know this or was he expecting a diminished Lilith?

[]:             Quote Post by The Abhorrent » Tue Jul 24, 2012 7:03 pm If Gendo needed Rei to be made whole once again, it's doubtful her soul would've been fragemented prior to the start of the series; even for the purpose of having Eva-00 be operational. There's no real indication of if Rei's fragmentation is deliberate or accidental (nor where or when for that matter), but it's fairly safe to assume it was a risk Gendo wouldn't have taken if fragmenting Rei's soul would end up being detrimental to his final objective. There's also the prospect that Rei's fragmentation, if deliberate, could've been motivated by control. Rei II shows far less initiative and is more obidient, potentially because her sense of self is lacking due to "not being whole"; in comparison, Rei III ultimately rebelled against him (almost vindicitively so). He may have been playing a bit of a gamble, in that he was hoping that Rei III hadn't yet regained her volition (or that one of the fragments died in the blast); in which case, he lost. But again, the fact the fragmentation occurred (and wasn't actively trying to reverse it) means that either Rei II or Rei III would've sufficed for his objective. Any Rei would be able to direct 3I, though the more "malleable" Rei II would be more likely to listen to him. Rei III, being whole once again, was more likely to go against him (but not necessarily gauranteed to do so).

[]:             Quote Post by Seoul Gamer » Fri Jul 27, 2012 4:14 pm The Abhorrent wrote: If Gendo needed Rei to be made whole once again, it's doubtful her soul would've been fragemented prior to the start of the series; even for the purpose of having Eva-00 be operational. There's no real indication of if Rei's fragmentation is deliberate or accidental (nor where or when for that matter), but it's fairly safe to assume it was a risk Gendo wouldn't have taken if fragmenting Rei's soul would end up being detrimental to his final objective. There's also the prospect that Rei's fragmentation, if deliberate, could've been motivated by control. Rei II shows far less initiative and is more obidient, potentially because her sense of self is lacking due to "not being whole"; in comparison, Rei III ultimately rebelled against him (almost vindicitively so). He may have been playing a bit of a gamble, in that he was hoping that Rei III hadn't yet regained her volition (or that one of the fragments died in the blast); in which case, he lost. But again, the fact the fragmentation occurred (and wasn't actively trying to reverse it) means that either Rei II or Rei III would've sufficed for his objective. Any Rei would be able to direct 3I, though the more "malleable" Rei II would be more likely to listen to him. Rei III, being whole once again, was more likely to go against him (but not necessarily gauranteed to do so).

[]:             Quote Post by Seele00TextOnly » Fri Jul 27, 2012 5:56 pm ...
